summary: In this interview, Sebastian Thrun discusses the origins of Google X and Waymo, his philosophy on moonshot thinking, and his views on management and AI. He emphasizes the importance of setting clear, non-negotiable milestones, protecting innovators from corporate bureaucracy, and maintaining an optimistic view of technology's ability to solve humanity's biggest problems.

principles.rationale: Written milestones act as a contractual document that provides absolute clarity for the team, eliminating arguments about what to build and allowing them to focus entirely on execution.
principles.source_quote: I wanted clarity about where we're going. And the best way to codify is was to set ourselves milestones... effectively became a contractual document where we really say this is the technical thing we have to accomplish

principles.statement: Technologically, almost anything can be done.
principles.rationale: Society is progressing rapidly, and less than 1% of interesting things have been invented. The barrier to solving massive problems is usually a lack of trying, not a lack of technological capability.
principles.source_quote: Technologically pretty much everything can be done. Uh almost nothing cannot be done. And that's actually a surprising thing because most of us in our mindset believe we we love the past invention.

principles.statement: Management is about removing roadblocks, not accountability.
principles.rationale: The role of a manager is to empower their people and handle administrative burdens so the team can focus on their work, rather than demanding updates and enforcing rules.
principles.source_quote: Management is not about accountability and all that stuff. Management is about removing roadblocks and really empowering your people.

principles.statement: Everything that can be made will be made.
principles.rationale: Technological progress is inevitable. The responsibility of technologists is not to try to stop invention, but to actively work to prevent the misuse of the things that are created.
principles.source_quote: First, everything that can be made will be made. Whether you say it should or should not be made is immaterial because it will be made. Our job is not to stop making things. Our job is to prevent the misuse of things that we make.

frameworks.name: Moonshot Ideation
frameworks.when_to_apply: When trying to invent something massively impactful or audacious.
steps: Pick a problem you deeply care about (e.g., a personal loss or societal issue).
Ask yourself if you can envision a technology that can solve this problem.
Work on it, trusting that technologically it can likely be solved if you try hard enough.
frameworks.source_quote: So the first thing is pick a problem you care about like your grandmother cares about like in my case the death of my friend Harold and then ask yourself can you envision a technology that can solve this problem and then work on it.

mental_models.name: The Skunkworks Model
mental_models.description: Isolating a small group of innovators from corporate distractions (management, finance, reporting) to focus entirely on solving a hard problem and building a new business.
mental_models.example: Setting up Google X with no finance people, a moratorium on PowerPoints, complete secrecy from the rest of Google, and no permanent lifetime researchers.

mental_models.name: The Farming Analogy (Technological Job Creation)
mental_models.description: Using the historical shift from agricultural labor to modern diverse professions to understand how AI will affect future employment. Technology destroys specific tasks but amplifies human capability, creating new, diverse jobs.
mental_models.example: Noting that while farming jobs massively disappeared due to technology, society gained massage therapists, pilots, and software engineers, making the world better off.

heuristics: Hire only when you are desperate.
Stop publishing papers if you want to build a product.

signature_quotes.text: Technologically pretty much everything can be done. Uh almost nothing cannot be done.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing advice for prospective moonshot takers.

signature_quotes.text: Everything that can be made will be made. Whether you say it should or should not be made is immaterial because it will be made. Our job is to prevent the misuse of things that we make.
signature_quotes.context: Addressing the ethical responsibility of technologists.

signature_quotes.text: Management is not about accountability and all that stuff. Management is about removing roadblocks and really empowering your people.
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on what he learned from his manager, Bill Coughran.

signature_quotes.text: You can have a thousand people reference your paper or a billion people use your product and you can only pick one. Which one is it going to be?
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the 'no publication' rule at Google X.

anti_patterns.description: Academic publishing in a product-focused innovation lab.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It puts researchers in a 'hamster wheel' of optimizing for personal brand and half-year conference cycles, which is inconsistent with the longevity required to carry a project through to a real product.

anti_patterns.description: PowerPoint updates and long reporting chains.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Every manager in the chain wants to add their own idea to take credit, which bloats the original pristine milestone and makes it impossible for the engineers at the bottom to execute.

anti_patterns.description: Filling headcount budgets just because they are available.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It misincentivizes managers to hire people quickly before the budget goes away, rather than waiting until they are truly desperate for a specific role, leading to unnecessary management overhead.
